question: Tell me about a time when you collaborated with data engineers to improve data pipelines or infrastructure.
spoken_answer: At Coinbase, the retail organization depended on a single metrics cube that had become bloated and fragile. I needed to partner with data engineers to make the pipeline faster without silently breaking business-critical metrics. Wrote scripts to trace retail metrics across upstream tables, dashboards, experiments, and owners. I also worked with data engineers to understand the proposed upstream change and identify exactly which downstream logic would break. We were able to support the pipeline improvement without breaking the retail metrics teams depended on.
follow_up_points: Back-tested the updated logic against existing metrics and flagged cases where the new table behavior changed business reads. | Coordinated the migration with DS and stakeholder owners so the engineering change could land safely. | The work also created a clearer dependency map for future metric changes, which reduced repeated firefighting.

question: Tell me about a time when you had to communicate complex technical findings to a non-technical audience. How did you ensure effective communication?
spoken_answer: At Intuit, I was leading the analytical work for TurboTax's end-to-end retention strategy. I needed to educate non-technical stakeholders on retention fundamentals, create a shared measurement framework, and get enough buy-in for them to join a centralized retention mission team. Aligned with my manager and directors upfront on what decisions the workshops needed to unlock. I also core concepts first, then the more nuanced cuts like tenure, SKU, and acquisition channel. The workshops gave the org a shared retention vocabulary and were strong enough that stakeholders volunteered to join the centralized mission team.
follow_up_points: For each analysis, led with the business takeaway and the decision implication instead of the method. | Repeated the same framework across sessions so stakeholders left with a common language, not just a one-time presentation. | That common framework became the basis for how TurboTax discussed retention strategy going forward.

question: Describe a situation where you had to present your findings to senior leadership. How did you prepare and what was the outcome?
spoken_answer: After Intuit shut down Mint and consolidated capabilities into Credit Karma, I found that users who had historically been active on both Mint and TurboTax were now retaining 1 to 2 points worse. My job was to quantify the retention gap, identify a realistic alternative inside the ecosystem, and present a recommendation senior leaders could act on. Measured the historical retention benefit of Mint engagement as a benchmark. I also built a lightweight matching framework to estimate the effect of Credit Karma plus TurboTax engagement versus Mint plus TurboTax engagement. The work directly informed the following tax year's retention strategy.
follow_up_points: Designed and ran an experiment on Credit Karma to test whether TurboTax messaging could recover part of the lost retention. | size of the gap, expected business value of the intervention, and the decision path forward. | The Credit Karma experiment drove about 35K incremental units and roughly $4M in incremental revenue, and the channel became one of the major ecosystem growth pillars for the season.

question: Give an example of when you had to translate technical results into business recommendations.
spoken_answer: At Intuit, I found that the Mint shutdown was creating a measurable retention gap for TurboTax among users who had historically engaged with both products. I needed to turn that analysis into a clear business recommendation rather than stopping at “there is a problem.”. Quantified the size of the retention loss and showed that doing nothing would leave a meaningful hole in the following season. I also tested whether Credit Karma could recreate part of Mint's retention flywheel through targeted TurboTax messaging. Leadership used that recommendation to make Credit Karma messaging a core retention lever for the next tax season.

question: Tell me about a time when you had to explain a technical project and ensure all stakeholders understood what you were conveying.
spoken_answer: At Intuit, many experiment teams were overstating success because early treatment gains were often driven by pull-forward rather than true incrementality. I needed to explain a new measurement approach to PMs, analysts, and leaders so they could understand when an experiment had created real value versus just shifted timing. “Did we grow the pie, or did we just move behavior earlier?”. I also used historical experiments to show the exact pattern of early lift followed by catch-up. That framing made the method easy to adopt across teams.
follow_up_points: Turned the methodology into a reproducible notebook with the same output structure every time, so teams saw the same decomposition across experiments. | Avoided jargon and only introduced the statistical details after the business intuition was clear. | It became the standard way TurboTax evaluated pull-forward-sensitive experiments, which improved decision quality and prevented overstated ROI calls.

question: Describe a situation where you needed to gain buy-in from skeptical stakeholders for a data science initiative.
spoken_answer: At Intuit, I wanted to run an experiment that used Credit Karma app real estate as a TurboTax retention lever. I needed to win buy-in from a partner team that believed this initiative helped us at their expense. Diagnosed that the resistance was not about the experiment mechanics; it was about incentives. I also reframed the analysis in their terms by quantifying the displacement cost to CK alongside the ecosystem upside. The CK team agreed to prioritize the experiment.
follow_up_points: Built scenarios that showed the retention gain for TurboTax outweighed the foregone value from that real estate. | Brought them a bounded test with clear success criteria instead of asking for open-ended support. | It drove about 35K incremental units and $4M in revenue, and the channel became a strategic retention pillar the following year.

question: Tell me about a time when you led a decision-making process within a data science team. How did you ensure everyone's voices were heard?
spoken_answer: At Intuit, a colleague and I were tasked with building the methodology to separate true experiment lift from pull-forward effects during tax season. We needed to converge quickly on one approach the team could trust and ship, without turning the process into an argument about statistical style. Let both lines of thinking get explored rather than forcing a decision up front. I also prototyped my simpler defector-model-based approach and asked her to pressure-test it against the more complex alternatives. We aligned on the simpler approach because it preserved decision quality without adding unnecessary complexity.
follow_up_points: Compared the methods on the same historical experiments so the team could evaluate tradeoffs on evidence, not preference. | Optimized for a method that was both credible statistically and easy for product teams to interpret. | That became the Lift vs Shift framework, and the process worked because everyone's perspective shaped the final solution.

question: Describe a project where you demonstrated leadership without formal authority.
spoken_answer: During my first tax season at Intuit, my manager moved into an IC role in the middle of the season just before the second April peak. I needed to keep KPI reporting and executive communication running during the busiest part of the season while fixing stakeholder complaints that the reporting had too many metrics, too little narrative, and no clear recommendations. Took ownership of the workstream even though the analysts and partner teams did not report to me. I also reworked the reporting structure so every update led with the business story, then the metrics, then the recommended next steps. We got through the second peak without disruption, the new reporting framework became the default for the team, and it helped drive decisions that contributed to roughly a 4 point increase in incremental retention for the loyalty program.
follow_up_points: Partnered with Finance and cross-functional analysts to align each report to the actual decisions leaders needed to make. | Set a steady reporting cadence and created clear accountability for follow-ups so the team stayed coordinated during peak season. | It was one of the clearest examples of me operating at staff scope before I had the title.

question: Tell me about a time when you mentored or coached junior data scientists. What was your approach?
spoken_answer: At Overjet, after we hired a new data scientist, I realized the team lacked a clear way to ramp DS hires quickly into the domain. I needed to help a newer data scientist get productive quickly while also raising the quality bar for future hires. Wrote a more rigorous interview and take-home challenge so we assessed the kind of judgment the role actually needed. I also during onboarding, paired with the new hire on early analyses and spent time explaining the clinical context behind the data, not just the code. The new hire ramped successfully and was able to take on meaningful DS work with less hand-holding, and the interview/onboarding materials continued to be used after I left.
follow_up_points: Gave feedback in working sessions rather than after the fact so they could see how I scoped problems, validated assumptions, and communicated tradeoffs. | Turned repeatable guidance into templates and examples so the coaching could scale beyond one person. | I would still want a stronger pure people-management example here, but this is the clearest coaching example from my experience.

question: Describe a situation where you had to manage differing priorities and opinions across teams.
spoken_answer: At Intuit, TurboTax wanted to use Credit Karma real estate for retention messaging, while CK leaders were protecting that same space for their own monetization goals. I needed to help both teams get to a decision without pretending the tradeoff did not exist. Made the conflict explicit instead of treating it as a communication issue alone. question: Describe a time when you worked in an ambiguous environment without clear guidelines. How did you approach it?
spoken_answer: At Overjet, a large dental client asked us to audit 20 clinics involved in a lawsuit and told us, in effect, “find the anomalies.” The ask was vague, the data was messy, and there was no established playbook for how to do this quickly and defensibly. I needed to turn an ambiguous legal and business question into a concrete analytical plan under a tight deadline. Over-utilization, under-utilization, documentation inconsistencies, and payer mix patterns. I also went directly to the raw schemas rather than trusting processed data because the stakes were too high. We delivered a defensible analysis on time, uncovered that the originally flagged providers were not actually the worst performers, and turned the work into the basis of a reusable product capability.
follow_up_points: Set up iterative review loops with the clinical team so we could validate the framing as we learned. | Narrowed the analysis to the questions we could answer credibly in the time available instead of trying to solve everything.

question: Tell me about a challenging data science problem you solved. Walk me through your approach from start to finish.
spoken_answer: At Coinbase, I was leading analysis for a high-visibility Portfolio Performance experiment when Bitcoin fell sharply just before the launch decision. I needed to isolate the feature's true effect quickly enough to make a launch recommendation on the original timeline. Split users into synthetic cohorts that were likely to differ in sensitivity to market volatility. I also checked balance within those cohorts so we were comparing like with like. The analysis showed the apparent harm was driven by market conditions, not the feature.
follow_up_points: Read the guardrails separately by cohort to separate product impact from macro conditions. | Pressure-tested the quantitative result with user interviews so the behavioral story matched the statistical one. | That let leadership proceed with the launch on schedule instead of making the wrong call because of a confounded read.

question: Describe a situation where you faced a problem outside your area of expertise. How did you handle it?
spoken_answer: At Intuit, I needed a matching-based causal inference approach that was fast enough for tax season decisions, but I did not come from a formal causal inference background and our centralized experts were not always available on the timeline I needed. I had to get good enough at the method to solve a real business problem without pretending to be a specialist I was not. Read the relevant research and reimplemented the core ideas in a lightweight form I could understand and test myself. I also validated the approach on historical experiments and compared the results against simpler baselines. The result was CEM-Lite, a practical matching tool the team adopted for retention work.
follow_up_points: Got feedback from more specialized teammates where I could, but kept ownership of the implementation and the decision framework. | Used the method only where I could explain its assumptions and defend the output. | The broader lesson for me was that when you are outside your comfort zone, you earn trust by learning fast and staying honest about the method's limits.

question: Tell me about a time when you had to balance competing priorities with limited resources and tight deadlines.
spoken_answer: At Overjet, a Delta Dental client needed a production-ready text service in two weeks to improve claim review specificity. I had to deliver a client-ready solution under a hard deadline without over-engineering it. Determine whether decay was actually mentioned for each tooth. I also chose a bag-of-words plus regex approach over a heavier NLP pipeline because it was faster, more interpretable, and easier for the clinical team to validate. We shipped on time, improved specificity by about 80 percent, and won a major annual contract.
follow_up_points: Built the service, added tests, and deployed it as an HTTP function on GCP. | Used the time saved to harden the logic and work through edge cases with the clinical team. | It was a good example of matching the technical solution to the business constraint.

question: Describe a time when things did not go as planned. How did you handle it?
spoken_answer: At Intuit, I proposed building retention-specific customer segments because I believed our existing segmentation was too coarse for the problems we were trying to solve. I had to decide whether to keep pushing a technically sound idea once I realized the organization was not ready to absorb it. Socialized the proposal and listened carefully to how stakeholders were actually using the current segmentation system. I also realized adoption of the existing segments was still incomplete, so a second segmentation layer would add complexity faster than value. The org got the retention targeting it needed with much faster adoption, and I learned that good strategy depends as much on organizational readiness as technical correctness.
follow_up_points: Parked the original project instead of forcing it through. | Proposed a lower-friction alternative: add retention heuristics to the existing segments rather than introducing an entirely new framework.

question: Tell me about a situation where you faced a challenging data quality issue. How did you identify and resolve it?
spoken_answer: At Overjet, I was asked to analyze five years of data across 20 dental clinics for a lawsuit-related fraud audit. I needed to identify the most damaging quality issues, clean the data in a clinically defensible way, and still deliver on a six-week legal timeline. Non-standard codes, duplicates, missing fields, and logically impossible records. I also worked from the underlying schemas instead of relying on pre-processed layers. We delivered a defensible cleaned dataset on time, the legal analysis changed the client's understanding of who the worst providers were, and the pipelines became the basis for a reusable chart-audit product feature.
follow_up_points: Built standardization and validation logic, then routed outputs to clinicians for iterative review. | Used the validation loop to refine the cleaning logic as we uncovered new edge cases.

question: Describe a time when you worked with a large, messy dataset. How did you preprocess and clean the data?
spoken_answer: As the first data scientist at Overjet, I had to analyze five years of patient records across 20 clinics using three different practice management systems. I needed to build preprocessing from scratch that standardized the systems into one analyzable dataset while keeping the output trustworthy enough for a high-stakes client decision. Reverse-engineered each source schema and mapped the fields to a common structure. I also standardized procedure codes to ADA-aligned definitions using lookup tables and clinical review. The cleaned dataset supported the fraud analysis on time and became reusable infrastructure for future customer work, which is exactly what I want from a messy-data project: a one-time answer and a longer-term asset.
follow_up_points: Added automated checks for duplicates, missingness, and impossible patterns. | Set up an iterative validation loop with the clinical team so that data cleaning and domain validation happened in parallel.

question: Give an example of when you had to debug a complex machine learning model or analysis.
spoken_answer: At Coinbase, I built a dataset for a high-visibility experiment and later realized one of my joins was exploding because I had missed a many-to-many relationship in the source tables. I needed to correct the analysis quickly, explain the mistake clearly, and make sure the same class of error could not pass silently again. Traced the inflation back to the join logic rather than trying to patch the aggregate output. I also told stakeholders immediately that the previously shared numbers were wrong and gave them a concrete timeline for the fix. The corrected analysis went out quickly, leadership appreciated the transparency, and the new validation checks permanently improved the QA path for that product's data.
follow_up_points: Rebuilt the query with the correct relationship handling and revalidated the outputs against source totals. | Added validation checks to the data mart so row explosions would be caught automatically in the future.

question: Tell me about a time when data inconsistencies threatened project success. What did you do?
spoken_answer: On the Overjet fraud audit, the core risk was that the inconsistencies across practice management systems would make the client and legal team mistrust the findings. I needed to make the data reliable enough that the conclusions would hold up under scrutiny. Identified the specific inconsistency classes instead of treating “messy data” as one abstract problem. I also standardized the data at the raw-schema layer. The project stayed credible, the findings changed the client's assumptions about provider behavior, and the work turned into reusable product infrastructure rather than a one-off cleanup.
follow_up_points: Added clinical validation loops so the cleaning logic was domain-credible, not just technically neat. | Narrowed the final analysis to the questions we could answer with high confidence.

question: Give an example of a project where you had to make trade-offs between model complexity and interpretability. How did you decide?
spoken_answer: At Overjet, a Delta Dental client wanted us to improve claim-review specificity by analyzing dental narratives. I had to choose between a more complex NLP pipeline and a simpler approach the team could build, validate, and debug under the deadline. Evaluated the complex option and realized the timeline, labeling needs, and validation burden made it a poor fit. I also chose a bag-of-words plus regex approach that the clinical team could inspect and approve. The simpler approach improved specificity by about 80 percent and won a major client contract.
follow_up_points: Built the service with unit and integration tests so the simple approach was also operationally reliable. | Prioritized interpretability because the end users needed to trust why a claim was flagged or not. | It was a strong reminder that the best model is the one that solves the real problem under the actual constraints.

question: Describe a time when you had to choose between different modeling approaches. What was your decision process?
spoken_answer: At Intuit, I was asked to build a leading-indicator framework that could predict season trajectory early enough for leadership to act. I needed to decide whether to stick with the elegant but weak initial approach or move to a messier model that better fit the signal. Tested the core assumption behind the simple correlation framework before over-investing further. I also found that the individual signals were real but too weak and unstable on their own. The final model gave leadership a usable early read on season trajectory.
follow_up_points: Switched to a random forest that combined W-2 availability, search trends, filing velocity, and competitive signals into one composite prediction. | Chose the more complex model because it materially improved actionability, while being disciplined about how I explained it to stakeholders. | The decision process mattered as much as the result: validate the simple idea first, then earn the complexity only if the data requires it.

question: Give an example of when you used feature engineering to significantly improve model performance.
spoken_answer: At Intuit, I owned the Veteran Cohort Model, which forecast end-of-season retention across roughly 300 cohort combinations. I needed to improve the forecast without making the model so complicated that Finance could no longer trust or use it. Used EDA and domain context to test whether income and refund-versus-balance-due status were missing signal. I also engineered those features into stable buckets that could be generated reliably every day during season. MAPE improved from about 1.1 percent to 0.2 percent, and the model became much better at flagging at-risk segments early enough for the business to act.
follow_up_points: Validated that these features explained why some cohorts, including seemingly tenured ones, still under-retained relative to the model's assumptions. | Expanded the cohorting logic so the model reflected those interactions rather than smoothing them away.

question: Describe a project where you had to balance the need for quick results with thoroughness.
spoken_answer: A large dental client needed an audit analysis in three weeks for an active lawsuit. I had to decide where we could safely move fast and where we could not afford shortcuts. Refused to rely on the standard post-processed data layer because I did not trust it for a legal use case. I also invested the first week in building raw-schema pipelines even though it was slower upfront. We delivered on time, the analysis held up for the client's legal needs, and the infrastructure later became part of our product.
follow_up_points: Made up time by running analysis and clinical validation in parallel rather than in serial handoffs. | Kept the question set narrow and defensible instead of chasing every possible pattern. | Spending on rigor in the right place let us move faster everywhere else.

question: Tell me about a time when a model or analysis didn't work as expected. How did you handle it?
spoken_answer: At Intuit, I initially built a leading-indicator framework on the assumption that individual early-season signals would each map cleanly to final conversion. I had to decide whether to force the original framework through or admit it was the wrong model for the problem. Tested the stability of each indicator across seasons and found the one-to-one relationships were not robust enough. I also stopped investing in the elegant but incorrect framework. The replacement model produced a usable early read for leadership, and the experience reinforced a habit I now trust: validate the core assumption before you build the machinery around it.
follow_up_points: Recast the problem as a composite-signal prediction task and built a random forest using the full signal set. | Updated stakeholders quickly so the project pivot felt deliberate rather than like a silent failure.

question: Describe a setback or failure in a data science project. What did you learn?
spoken_answer: At Coinbase, I shared a dataset with leadership that turned out to be wrong because one of my joins exploded on a many-to-many relationship I had not handled properly. I needed to recover quickly and, more importantly, handle the mistake in a way that preserved trust. Owned the mistake immediately instead of waiting for someone else to find it. I also rebuilt and revalidated the analysis from the source tables. The numbers were corrected quickly and stakeholder trust remained intact because I was transparent.
follow_up_points: Added QA checks so the same issue would be caught automatically in the future. | Reflected on why I missed it in the first place rather than treating it as a one-off accident. | The lesson was simple: once you know something is wrong, delay only makes the problem worse.

question: Give an example of when you received critical feedback. How did you respond?
spoken_answer: At Intuit, I was supporting a director with a high volume of ad-hoc retention requests and had slipped into a reactive mode where I answered the question in front of me instead of shaping the decision. My manager gave me direct feedback that I was behaving like an order-taker rather than a strategic senior data scientist. Built a tracker of ad-hoc asks so I could see the real pattern of needs rather than treating each request as unique. I also started conversations by asking what decision the stakeholder was trying to make. That feedback changed how I work with stakeholders.
follow_up_points: Pushed back using prior work when requests duplicated earlier analysis. | Began proposing scoped answers instead of accepting vague asks at face value. | I moved from reactive support to strategic partnership, and that shift was one of the clearest contributors to my later promotion to Staff Data Scientist.

question: Tell me about a mistake you made and how you recovered from it.

I needed to correct the mistake fast and handle the stakeholder communication well enough that we did not lose trust. Alerted stakeholders as soon as I confirmed the issue instead of trying to quietly fix it first. I also rebuilt the query and validated the corrected output against source-level checks. follow_up_points: Shared the corrected timeline and root cause transparently. | Added validation checks to prevent the same failure mode from recurring. | I recovered by being faster and more honest than the mistake itself.

question: Describe a time when you had to pivot your approach due to unexpected results.
spoken_answer: At Intuit, I proposed a new retention-specific segmentation framework because I believed the current segments were not nuanced enough for retention work. I needed to decide what to do once stakeholder feedback showed that adoption of the existing segmentation system was already incomplete. Treated the feedback as signal rather than resistance to push through. I also reframed the problem from “build a better segmentation framework” to “improve retention targeting with minimal adoption cost.”. The updated approach got immediate adoption and delivered the targeting value the business needed without creating a second system nobody would use consistently.
follow_up_points: Parked the original framework and instead layered retention heuristics into the segments people already used. | Explained the pivot explicitly so stakeholders understood this was a strategic simplification, not a retreat.

question: Tell me about a time when you disagreed with a team member or manager about a technical approach. How did you resolve it?
spoken_answer: At Intuit, a colleague and I disagreed on how to measure pull-forward effects in tax-season experiments. We needed to resolve the disagreement quickly enough to ship a method the business could rely on during season. Avoided turning it into a theoretical debate. I also built a prototype of the simpler approach and compared it against the more complex alternatives on the same historical experiments. We aligned on the simpler method because it preserved accuracy while being much easier to operationalize.
follow_up_points: decision quality and interpretability for stakeholders. | Used her exploration of the alternative methods to pressure-test whether the simpler approach was cutting corners. | That became the Lift vs Shift framework, and the disagreement improved the result rather than slowing it down.

question: Tell me about a time when you and another team had competing priorities. How did you navigate it?
spoken_answer: TurboTax wanted Credit Karma app real estate for retention messaging, while CK leaders were optimizing that same space for their own monetization goals. I needed to create a path where we could test the retention opportunity without pretending CK was not giving something up. Quantified both sides of the tradeoff instead of arguing values. I also reframed the discussion around ecosystem value rather than one team winning. That approach got the experiment prioritized, and the results justified the investment.
follow_up_points: Proposed a bounded experiment so the tradeoff could be learned, not debated abstractly. | Presented the analysis in CK's metrics and incentives, not just TurboTax's. | More importantly, it gave the teams a better way to navigate shared-surface conflicts going forward.

question: Tell me about a time when you used data science to drive a significant business decision or outcome.
spoken_answer: After the Mint shutdown, I discovered a retention gap for TurboTax among users who had historically engaged with Mint. I needed to turn that insight into a viable business decision that could recover value inside the existing ecosystem. Quantified the retention gap and showed it was not just noise. question: Tell me about a time when you had to quickly learn a new tool, technique, or domain.
spoken_answer: At Intuit, I needed a fast matching-based causal inference workflow for tax-season decisions, but I did not have formal training in that area and could not wait on a specialized team every time. I had to learn the technique quickly enough to use it responsibly on real business problems. Read the research on coarsened exact matching and rebuilt the method in a lightweight, practical form. I also tested it repeatedly on historical experiments and edge cases to understand where it held up and where it did not. That became CEM-Lite, a method my team adopted for fast causal reads during season.
follow_up_points: Added only the pieces that were necessary for the retention use cases we cared about. | Documented the assumptions so the team could use it correctly, not as a black box. | It was a good example of learning something quickly and still being disciplined about where it was appropriate.

question: Tell me about a time when you had to adapt your analysis approach due to changing requirements.
spoken_answer: When I was building an early-season leading indicator framework at Intuit, the original requirement was effectively “give us something interpretable enough for leadership to act on early.” My first approach optimized too hard for interpretability. I had to adapt once it became clear that the simple one-signal-to-one-outcome model was not strong enough to support the decision needs. Tested the initial assumptions instead of just decorating the framework further. I also recognized that the real signal was distributed across multiple variables. The adapted model produced a usable season read, and the bigger lesson was not to confuse a cleaner story with a better analysis.
follow_up_points: Pivoted to a random forest model that combined the leading signals into one prediction. | Reworked the communication so the business still got an actionable explanation even though the method itself was less elegant.

question: Tell me about a time when you had to consider ethical implications in your data analysis or model development.
spoken_answer: At Intuit, sub-50K income filers and many Gen Z users were a fast-growing segment, and the business pressure was to monetize them aggressively. question: Describe a situation where you identified or addressed potential bias in data or models.
spoken_answer: At Intuit, our Veteran Cohort Model used very tenured customers as retention anchors. I needed to determine whether the model was effectively biased toward the majority retention pattern and missing vulnerable segments. Sliced the forecast errors by income and by refund-versus-balance-due status instead of only looking at aggregate performance. I also found that low-income and balance-due users were being modeled too optimistically because the veteran-anchor assumption did not hold for them. The updated model materially improved forecast accuracy and, more importantly, stopped smoothing away risk for the very segments the business most needed to understand.
follow_up_points: Added those segment features into the model so the higher-risk groups were represented explicitly. | Revalidated performance both overall and by subgroup.

question: Give an example of how you've ensured fairness and privacy in your work.
spoken_answer: At Overjet, I was leading a fraud audit built on highly sensitive patient dental records that could end up being used in legal proceedings. I needed to decide what privacy standard to enforce even though it created more work and was not explicitly required. Kept each clinic's data fully separate rather than creating one pooled dataset. I also anonymized patient identifiers in every notebook and every output shared with the clinical team. We still delivered on time, and the work never put patient data at unnecessary risk.
follow_up_points: Built separate pipelines per clinic so there was no accidental cross-contamination of records. | Accepted the extra operational overhead because the downside risk to patients was too high. | For me, privacy only means something if you protect it when the client is not asking you to.

question: What's a non-negotiable for you?
spoken_answer: A non-negotiable for me is a work environment where people do not feel safe or respected enough to contribute. If I notice that a team dynamic is excluding people or making them internalize disrespect, I feel responsible for acting on it rather than waiting for a formal complaint. In Code for Sierra Leone, I noticed female coders were not being treated as equally welcome, even though nobody had made an explicit complaint. I reset expectations directly with the team and made the code of conduct real, not decorative. I also at Intuit, when a colleague was getting passive-aggressive pushback from an engineer, I helped her reframe the issue for what it was: a respect problem, not a communication flaw on her part. That is my non-negotiable: if people cannot contribute safely and with dignity, the team is already underperforming.
follow_up_points: In both cases, I acted before the behavior calcified into “just how things are.” | I would rather confront that early than optimize around it later.

question: How do you operate in a fast-paced, high-intensity work environment?
spoken_answer: I operate well in that kind of environment because I rely on a few habits rather than trying to be heroic. My goal is to keep output and decision quality high even when context is moving quickly. Ruthlessly prioritize, usually with a simple weekly ranking of what matters most. I also break big deliverables into smaller increments that ship value early. That combination has let me handle tax-season work, live experiments, and executive asks without losing the strategic thread.
follow_up_points: Over-invest in written communication because clear notes prevent more rework than most meetings do. | Keep a calm operating cadence so urgency does not become chaos. | Speed matters, but clarity matters just as much.

question: Who do you respect/admire but disagree with their values?
spoken_answer: I do not think I really admire someone as a person if I fundamentally disagree with their values. The tension is that her books were formative for me, but her public stance on trans people contradicts many of the values those books taught me about dignity and belonging. I separate the impact a work had on me from loyalty to the person who made it. I do not feel a need to defend her in order to acknowledge that the books mattered to me. So my answer is: the work influenced me, but I do not admire her values.
follow_up_points: If anything, the contradiction strengthened my own commitment to judge people by how they treat vulnerable groups, not by the cultural status they hold. | I think holding that distinction clearly is healthier than forcing false consistency.

question: Describe the experience you're most proud to have worked on.
spoken_answer: The experience I am most proud of is miKashBoks, a mobile money savings platform I helped build in Sierra Leone for subsistence farmers. I helped run the early design work and shape a product that would actually fit the context rather than assume smartphone access or ideal infrastructure. Went into farming communities to understand how people stored money, how they thought about risk, and what would block adoption. I also helped design a USSD-based workflow that fit low-connectivity, low-literacy settings. It is the project I am proudest of because it is real, live, and useful.
follow_up_points: Focused on the operational realities of trust, community distribution, and redemption, not just the interface. | Kept the design anchored to a real user problem rather than donor optics. | It mattered to people's lives in a direct way, not just to a dashboard or a metric.

question: "Do the simple thing that works" — When have you chosen simplicity over complexity?
spoken_answer: At Overjet, a major dental client needed a production-ready service in two weeks to improve specificity in claim review using dentist narrative text. I had to choose a solution that would work under the deadline and that the clinical team could actually validate. Rejected a heavier NLP pipeline because the data, timeline, and interpretability needs did not support it. I also built a bag-of-words plus regex system to detect decay mentions and map them to the right tooth. Specificity improved by about 80 percent and the client signed a major annual contract.
follow_up_points: Had the clinical team vet the patterns so the logic was grounded in the domain. | Deployed it as a tested Python HTTP service on GCP. | Simplicity won because it fit the real constraint set better than sophistication would have.

question: "Be good to our users" — When have you prioritized user impact in your work?
spoken_answer: At Intuit, I found that first-year returners and sub-50K Gen Z filers were meaningfully under-retaining, yet the organization was still often treating them as upsell opportunities rather than users whose trust we needed to earn. question: "Be helpful, honest, and harmless" — Tell me about a time you admitted you were wrong.
spoken_answer: At Coinbase, I shared an experiment dataset with leadership before realizing one of the joins had exploded because I had missed a many-to-many relationship. I needed to correct the mistake and do it in a way that protected trust rather than trying to look less wrong. Told stakeholders immediately that the prior numbers were wrong. I also explained the root cause clearly and gave a concrete timeline for the corrected output. The correction was fast, the trust cost stayed low because I was transparent, and the QA process improved permanently.
follow_up_points: Rebuilt and revalidated the dataset from the source tables. | Added validation checks so the same failure mode would be caught automatically next time. | The main lesson was that once you know you are wrong, speed and honesty matter more than self-protection.

question: Tell me about a time when you made a safety-first decision in a project.
spoken_answer: At Overjet, I led a fraud audit built on highly sensitive patient records that could end up in legal proceedings. I needed to decide whether to optimize for speed or add safeguards that would slow us down but materially reduce privacy risk. Kept each clinic's data fully separated rather than pooling everything into one easier workflow. I also anonymized patient identifiers in every analysis artifact shared outside the narrowest need-to-know circle. We still delivered on time and never put patient data at unnecessary risk.
follow_up_points: Built separate pipelines even though it added real overhead to an already tight timeline. | Accepted the cost because exposing patient records in a legal context was not an acceptable tradeoff. | It was a good reminder that safety means little if it disappears under deadline pressure.

question: Tell me about a time you had to make a difficult decision.
spoken_answer: While leading a pilot of IBM Africa's infectious disease platform in Sierra Leone, I saw that the system was impressing donors but frustrating health workers.
